anchorText: No pain felt she;        I am quite sure she felt no pain

levels.body: These lines feature repetition and a chiasmus, and the speaker shifts from Porphyria’s (alleged) sensations to the first person singular and his own perspective to transmit his own beliefs about her.

The first line “No pain felt she” can be read as an objective statement. However, by repeating the statement along with the words “I am quite sure” the speaker exposes the fact that the statement is subjective; he moreover he also shifts the focus from her (“felt she”) to “no pain”. This second line depicts the speaker’s take-over of power over Porphyria. He not only ends her life, he also determines how she feels in an attempt to also reassure himself of her sensation. He speaks on behalf of Porphyria while she has no space to voice her opinion or even to protest against his actions. 
The repetition also emphasizes that the speaker is personally convinced, or rather trying to convince himself, that Porphyria felt no pain and, therefore, supports the euphemistic depiction of Porphyria's death where the speaker avoids the gruesome details of the murder in an evasive manner.
